mappings.rationaleAI: Core perceptual psychology covering attention (A), vision (B.2, E, F), Gestalt organization (C.3), constancy (C.3.c), and illusions (G).
Retinal anatomy addresses retinal work (B.2.a), transduction (B.2.b), visual perception (B.2.c), cortical responses (B.2.d), depth perception (E.2), and motion cues (F.1).
Cortical sensory processing addresses visual cortical responses (B.2.d), attention physiology (A.5), and neurological hallucination factors (G.2.a).
Neural attention and perception address attention theories (A.2), physiological mechanisms (A.5), information theory (A.6), and cortical responses (B.2.d).
Attention mechanisms address attention definition (A.1), and altered states relate to hallucinations (G.2).
Hearing mechanisms address ear structures (B.3.a) and auditory processing (B.3.b).
Physics of light provides the basis for understanding vision (B.2).
Pain perception addresses the sensory experience of pain (B.4.d).
Olfactory processing addresses the olfactory sense (B.8).
Sensory physiology addresses attention mechanisms (A.5), sensation (B), kinesthesis (B.5), and vestibular function (B.6).
Sensory organs address ear structures (B.3.a) and vestibular apparatus (B.6).
Cross-modal experiences address perceptual individual differences (C.4), sense interrelations (E.3), and illusory types (G.1).
Attention-memory relationship addresses attention determinants (A.4).
Thinking-perceiving boundaries address perceptual functioning differences (C.4).
Cognitive psychology addresses attention (A), perceptual organization (C), and illusions (G).
Wave phenomena provide physical basis for vision (B.2) and audition (B.3).
Music processing addresses auditory processing (B.3.b) and time perception (D.2).
Dream mechanisms address hallucinations (G.2) and neurological factors (G.2.a).
Sensory processing differences address perceptual functioning variations (C.4).
Mental disturbance and hallucinations address hallucinatory experience (G.2).
Emotions' role in attention addresses attention determinants (A.4).
Drug effects address time perception factors (D.3) and drug-induced hallucinations (G.2).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationaleAI: Covers circadian regulation through melatonin and factors affecting time perception including sleep deprivation effects on human functioning, directly relevant to factors affecting time perception such as drugs and altered states.

Freud's theories of the unconscious, dream interpretation, and psychoanalysis address unconscious influences as determinants of attention and types of hallucinatory experience through the lens of wish fulfillment and the unconscious.
Schizophrenia characterized by hallucinations and delusions directly addresses types of hallucinatory experience and neurological factors in hallucinations, including brain imaging evidence.

Covers social suggestion and interpersonal influence as determinants of attention, and social psychological insights into group perception relevant to social and interpersonal aspects of space perception.

Covers hormonal regulation affecting appetite control and circadian adjustment, relevant to factors affecting taste and olfactory sensitivity, and the physiological mechanisms underlying sensory experience.

Covers major therapeutic approaches for conditions involving hallucinatory and delusional experience including cognitive and behaviour therapy, directly relevant to types of hallucinatory experience and their treatment.

CBT's fundamental principle that thoughts, mental events, and behaviours influence emotional states addresses stimulus-response theories of attention and central perceptual problems relating cognition to perception.
